title: Carved birch kuksa
subtitle: A Scandinavian drinking cup carved from a green birch block or burl
excerpt: A kuksa is a personal drinking cup carved from a single block of green birch. The challenge is the deep, even bowl: it must hold a cup of liquid while keeping walls and base of uniform thickness. More hook-knife work than a spoon, and considerably more patience. A third or fourth carving project for someone comfortable with axe and hook knife.
sourceNotes: Kuksa cup forms documented in Scandinavian folk-craft traditions; Sami birch-burl carving practices recorded in Swedish and Finnish ethnographic collections from the 19th century.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled wood still high in moisture content. Green birch is significantly softer than seasoned and is the correct material for deep bowl carving; the cup form must be established before the wood dries and hardens.

A rounded knotty growth on the trunk or branch of a tree, characterised by tightly swirling grain. Burl wood resists splitting when dried into a cup form and produces distinctive figure in the finished piece.
A short section of branch or block prepared for carving. A kuksa billet should be thick enough in both dimensions to allow for a full cup-depth hollow with walls remaining 6 to 8 mm thick.

A curved carving knife ground on the inside of the curve, used to hollow a cup or bowl. For a kuksa, a hook knife with a tight, deep curve is needed to reach the bottom of the cavity without the handle obstructing the rim.

projectSchedule.title: Axe and hollow the cup
projectSchedule.body: Rough outside profile with the axe, sloyd the exterior, begin hollow with brace-and-bit if available then refine with hook knife. A long session.

projectSchedule.title: Sand and oil after slow drying
projectSchedule.body: After two weeks in a paper bag to dry slowly, sand to 240 grit and apply raw linseed oil.

projectSchedule.title: Oil cured: ready for use
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks after oiling the raw linseed oil has cured. The kuksa is ready for use.

content.text: A kuksa is a personal drinking cup carved from a single block of
green
birch. Traditional Sami kuksat were carved from birch
burls

: dense, knotty growths that resist cracking and produce swirling figure in the finished cup. A block of straight-grained green birch of sufficient diameter is a more accessible alternative for most carvers. The challenge is not the outside form, which is simple, but the deep bowl: it must hold a cup of liquid while keeping walls of consistent thickness all the way round.
What you need: wood
A green birch
billet

, 12 to 14 cm long, 10 to 12 cm in diameter. The billet must be thick enough in both dimensions to allow the bowl hollow plus 6 to 8 mm of wall on all sides. A burl section at the same dimensions is preferable if one is available. Alder and cherry are good alternatives at the same size.

Birch dries quickly and can split if exposed to rapid air movement. Store an unworked billet with the end grain coated in PVA if it cannot be carved within a few days. Work green, and dry slowly in a paper bag once carved.
What you need: tools
A carving axe for roughing the outside. A sloyd knife for the exterior form and the handle. A
hook knife

with a deep, tight curve for the bowl interior. A leather strop for all three. A 1000-grit waterstone for the hook knife's inside bevel. A brace and bit (optional) to remove waste from the bowl centre before the hook knife. Sandpaper in 180 and 240 grit. Raw linseed oil.
The work
Step 1: Mark and rough the outside

Mark the cup profile on the billet with pencil. The cup body is roughly oval in plan, about 8 cm wide and 10 cm long. The handle extends from one short end: a simple flat ear or a more elaborate carved grip, 4 to 5 cm long. Mark the rim line and the base line. Axe away waste from the sides to produce the plan outline, then axe the base to the correct thickness (the base plus bowl floor together should be about 10 to 12 mm).
Step 2: Sloyd the outside and handle
Refine the outside of the cup body with
push cuts

. The cup exterior should taper very slightly from rim to base. Shape the handle to a comfortable thumb-fitting grip. The outside should be close to final form before the hollow is started; working inside a partially complete exterior is harder to check.
Step 3: Rough out the bowl hollow

If using a brace and bit: drill a series of holes in the waste area inside the marked rim, to a depth equal to the intended bowl depth (about 40 to 45 mm). The drill waste roughes out the centre faster than the hook knife alone. If working without a drill, begin directly with the hook knife.

With the hook knife, work from the centre outward toward the rim in short scooping cuts. Rotate the cup in the hand to keep the cutting angle consistent. The hook knife on green birch cuts well; on a burl, the grain may resist in unexpected directions. The target depth is 40 to 45 mm at the centre, tapering to leave 6 to 8 mm of wall at the rim. Check wall thickness frequently by pressing from outside.
Step 4: Refine the interior
Use a
stop cut

around the inside of the rim to define a clean wall edge. Work the bowl floor level with repeated light passes. The bowl should be consistent in depth: a low-angled bench light will show ridges and hollows by shadow. The walls should be smooth and close to uniform thickness before drying; it is very difficult to correct an uneven wall in dried birch.
Step 5: Slow-dry in a paper bag

Place the cup in a paper bag, loosely folded shut, and leave in a room-temperature location away from heat and sun for two weeks. The paper bag allows the wood to breathe while slowing surface drying relative to the core, which reduces the cracking risk. Birch cups dried too quickly often split at the base or along the grain at the rim. Do not rush this step.
Step 6: Sand the exterior
Once dry, sand the exterior through 180 and 240 grit. The interior is left with its knife-finished surface; the exterior is sanded for comfort and appearance. Wipe away all dust before oiling.
Finishing

Apply raw linseed oil inside and out, working the oil into the bowl with a fingertip. Allow to soak for one hour, then wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before use. The cup is not food-safe until the cure is complete.
Variations
Source a birch burl from a timber supplier or a fallen tree. Burl grain resists cracking during drying and produces a cup with unique swirling figure.
Make the handle a simple thumb-loop grip by carving through the ear with a detail knife once the exterior is shaped.
Add kolrosing decoration to the outside of the cup body after sanding: a simple border or repeat pattern in the tradition of Scandinavian domestic woodwork.
Care
Rinse in warm water and dry immediately. Never dishwasher, never soak. Re-oil every three to four months. A kuksa in regular use will darken gradually; this is normal and enhances the grain figure.

items.symptom: The cup splits along the grain during drying
items.cause: Drying was too rapid, or the wall thickness is uneven and the thinner side dried faster than the thicker.
items.fix: Re-wet the crack with a damp cloth and close it under clamping pressure overnight; on green birch this sometimes works. For future work, dry in a paper bag and check wall thickness consistency before setting aside.

items.symptom: The bowl floor is not level; one side is deeper than the other
items.cause: The hook knife cut deeper on one side during the initial roughing passes.
items.fix: Check floor level with a flat-faced card laid across the rim. Mark the high points with pencil. Take additional passes on the high side until the floor is flat.

items.symptom: The hook knife cannot reach the centre of the bowl
items.cause: The bowl rim diameter is too small for the hook knife's handle radius.
items.fix: Use the drill option to remove centre waste first, reducing the depth the hook knife must travel from the rim. Alternatively, use a straight detail knife to score the centre floor and lever out small pieces.
